attributes.stem: A previously well 65-year-old woman presents with a two day history of colicky abdominal pain and vomiting. She has not been able to tolerate any oral intake for the last 24 hours. she has a history of chronic kidney disease on haemodialysis. On examination her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg and pulse 100/min. Her BMI is 31. Her abdomen is distended, but soft. The bowel sounds are increased and tinkling. In her right groin there is a 3 cm tender swelling immediately below and lateral to the pubic tubercle. Her serum biochemistry results are shown. (Image)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic investigation?
options: Fine needle aspiration of the lump
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a two-day history of colicky abdominal pain and vomiting and the physical examination is suggestive of an intestinal obstruction. This diagnosis is supported by the finding of increased and tinkling bowel sounds, paired with a 3 cm tender swelling in her right groin. The location of this swelling (below and lateral to the pubic tubercle) is consistent with an incarcerated femoral hernia. The experienced clinician will appreciate that the tenderness of this swelling implies likely ischaemia and the patient needs prompt surgical intervention. In such hands, any form of imaging will be superfluous. However, in real life this patient will go through a number of investigations leading up to final decision-making.

Most patients with an intestinal obstruction present acutely with an abrupt onset of colicky abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal distention. The most important risk factors for an intestinal obstruction include:

  • Abdominal or pelvic surgery (e.g., due to adhesion formation)

  • Abdominal wall or groin hernia

  • Inflammatory disease of the bowel (e.g., ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease)

  • Gastrointestinal malignancy.

The first consideration in a patient like this is the physical examination. After the examination has been completed a laboratory evaluation should be completed. For patients who present with significant abdominal pain laboratory evaluation typically includes a complete blood count and electrolyte panel (including blood urea nitrogen and creatinine). Although not specific for a diagnosis of intestinal obstruction, these studies facilitate the assessment of the severity of hypovolaemia and metabolic abnormalities. As the patient has been vomiting, with a poor oral intake, the only investigation required will be some baseline laboratory investigations. It is highly likely that this patient will require intravenous fluid replacement prior to surgery and her needs will be guided by the serum electrolyte picture.

Although intestinal obstruction may be suspected based on a combination of clinical examination findings, various risk factors, and symptoms, abdominal imaging is usually required to confirm the diagnosis. The preferred imaging modality to confirm the diagnosis of intestinal obstruction is computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast. In this case, the serum biochemistry shows impaired renal function and this would exclude any contrast studies.

While imaging is often an essential part of the workup in a suspected bowel obstruction, the cause of the suspected intestinal obstruction is evident in this case - an obstructed femoral hernia. A plain abdominal X-ray would confirm the presence of intestinal obstruction, but would not define the cause. In this case, an ultrasound examination of the groin would confirm the presence of the femoral hernia. This imaging investigation can usually be done in the emergency department and is more convenient than a CT scan, and in these circumstances would provide all the information that is required.

Whilst urinalysis will be performed as part of the routine evaluation of the patient, it is unlikely to have any diagnostic value in this case. Any thoughts of doing a fine needle aspiration of this lump would not be sound medical practice.
learningPoints: If any emergency imaging investigation is to be done in a case of intestinal obstruction due to an abdominal wall hernia, then an ultrasound will usually be the most expedient.
references: Bordeianou, L. Etiologies, clinical manifestations, and diagnosis of mechanical small bowel obstruction in adults [Internet] Uptodate.com 2022 [cited 16 Apr 2022]. Available from < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/etiologies-clinical-manifestations-and-diagnosis-of-mechanical-small-bowel-obstruction-in-adults>

ten Broek, R.P.G., Krielen, P., Di Saverio, S. et al. Bologna guidelines for diagnosis and management of adhesive small bowel obstruction (ASBO): 2017 update of the evidence-based guidelines from the world society of emergency surgery ASBO working group. World J Emerg Surg 13, 24 (2018). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13017-018-0185-2
